52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
177	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
178	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
179	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
180	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
181	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
182	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
183	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
184	
185	## Reading references
186	
187	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
188	
189	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
190	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
191	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
192	
193	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
194	
195	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
196	
197	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
198	
199	Conditions for dispatch:
200	
201	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
202	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
203	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
204	
205	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
206	
207	```
208	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
209	  --name reference-retriever \
210	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
211	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
212	  --write-domain 'references/**'
213	```
214	
215	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
216	
217	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
218	
219	## Workflow
220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.
222	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
223	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
224	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
225	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
226	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
227	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as a $g$-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	$\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)$
20	on the category of $k$-schemes. Representing $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve $C \times_k T \to T$, which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras
23	$\mathcal{A}$, the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	$\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$. This chapter packages the construction
25	of $\underline{\Spec}_X(-)$ and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of $J$ (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}[Quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra]
40	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
42	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
43	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
44	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
45	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
46	  % "Here $S$ is a scheme, and $\mathcal{A}$ is a quasi-coherent
47	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebra. This means that $\mathcal{A}$ is a
48	  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_S$-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
49	  %  $\mathcal{O}_S$-module."
50	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
51	  Let $X$ be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras} is a
52	  sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras (i.e. an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
53	  equipped with $\mathcal{O}_X$-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
54	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
55	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is quasi-coherent. We write $\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)$ for the
56	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebras and
57	  $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra morphisms.
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
61	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
62	
63	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open $U = \Spec R \subseteq X$ one
64	sets $\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ regarded as an $R$-algebra, and the local pieces
65	glue compatibly because $\mathcal{A}$ is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
66	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for an inclusion
67	$V = \Spec S \subseteq U$ gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
68	
69	\begin{theorem}[Relative spectrum exists]
70	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
72	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
73	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
74	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
75	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
76	  % "In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
77	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
78	  %  $$
79	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
80	  %  $$
81	  %  with the following properties:
82	  %  \begin{enumerate}
83	  %  \item for every affine open $U \subset S$ there exists an isomorphism
84	  %  $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$, and
85	  %  \item for $U \subset U' \subset S$ affine open the composition
86	  %  $$
87	  %  \xymatrix{
88	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
89	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
90	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
91	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
92	  %  }
93	  %  $$
94	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-inclusion} above.
95	  %  \end{enumerate}
96	  %  Moreover, $\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})$
97	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over $S$."
98	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
99	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
100	  Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{A}$ a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-algebra
101	  (Definition~\ref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
102	  $\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})$ together with an affine morphism
103	  $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X$ such that
104	  \begin{enumerate}
105	    \item for every affine open $U \subseteq X$, there is an isomorphism
106	      $i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))$ over $U$;

407	\section{Lean encoding}
408	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
409	
410	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
411	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
412	$U \subseteq X$ with $U = \Spec R$ and $A := \mathcal{A}(U)$ regarded as an
413	$R$-algebra, the local piece is $\Spec A$ with structure morphism $\Spec A \to \Spec R = U$
414	induced by the algebra unit $R \to A$. Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
415	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
416	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of $\mathcal{A}$ via
417	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
418	$\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)$ for $V = \Spec S \subseteq U$).
419	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
420	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
421	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
422	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
423	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
424	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
425	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
426	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
427	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_base_change}) and functoriality
428	(Theorem~\ref{thm:relative_spec_functorial}) are derived from the universal property by
429	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
430	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.

1266	\section{The several Picard functors}\label{sc:Pfctrs}
1267	\renewcommand{\theequation}{\thethm.\arabic{equation}}
1268	Our first job is to identify a likely candidate for the functor of
1269	points of the Picard scheme.  In fact, there are several reasonable such
1270	Picard functors, and each one is more likely to be representable than
1271	the preceding.  In this section, they all are formally introduced and
1272	compared.
1273	
1274	\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1275	 The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1276	 category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1277	abelian groups defined by the formula
1278		$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1279	\end{dfn}
1280	
1281	The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
1282	sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
1283		$$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$
1284	 Hence, given a covering family $\{T_i\to T\}$ in the Zariski topology,
1285	the \'etale topology, or any other Grothendieck topology on the category
1286	of $S$-schemes, there is no harm, when we consider the sheaf associated
1287	to $\Pic_X$, in working simply with the single map $T'\to T$ where
1288	$T':=\coprod T_i$.  And doing so lightens the notation, making for
1289	easier reading. Therefore, we do so throughout, calling $T'\to T$ simply
1290	a {\it covering\/} in the given topology.
1291	
1292	The absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never a separated presheaf in
1293	the Zariski topology.  Indeed, take an $S$-scheme $T$ that carries an
1294	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ such that $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.  (For
1295	example, take $T:=\I P^1_X$ and $\mc N:=\mc O_{\!T}(1)$.  Then the diagonal
1296	map $X\to X\x X$ induces a section $g$ of $f_T$; that is, $gf_T=1_T$.
1297	Hence $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.)  Now, there exists a Zariski covering
1298	$T'\to T$ such that the pullback $\mc N|T'$ is trivial; here, $T'$ is
1299	simply the disjoint union of the subsets in a suitable ordinary open
1300	covering of $T$.  So the pullback $f^*_{T'}\mc N|X_{T'}$ is trivial too.
1301	Thus $\Pic_X(T)$ has a nonzero element whose restriction is zero in
1302	$\Pic_X(T')$.
1303	
1304	According to descent theory, every representable functor is a sheaf in
1305	the Zariski topology \emdash in fact, in the \'etale and fppf
1306	topologies as well.  Therefore, the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never
1307	representable.  So, in the hope of obtaining a representable functor
1308	that differs as little as possible from $\Pic_X$, we now define a
1309	sequence of three successively more promising relative functors.
1310	
1311	\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1312	  The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1313		$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1314	 Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1315	topologies  by
1316		$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1317		\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1318	 \end{dfn}
1319	
1320	We now have a sequence of five Picard functors, and each one maps
1321	naturally into the next.  So each one maps naturally into any of its
1322	successors.  If the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
1323	then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
1324	associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
1325	the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
1326	check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
1327	associated to the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$, as well as to the
1328	relative Picard functor $\Pic_{X/S}$.
1329	
1330	Since $\Pic_{X/S}$ is not a priori a sheaf, it is remarkable that it is
1331	representable so often in practice.
1332	
1333	Note that, for every $S$-scheme $T$, each $T$-point of
1334	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$, or element of $\Pic_{(X/S)\fpqc}(T)$, is
1335	represented by an invertible sheaf $\mc L'$ on $X_{T'}$ for some
1336	fppf-covering $T'\to T$.    Moreover, there must
1337	be an fppf-covering $T''\to T'\x_T T'$ such that the two pullbacks of
1338	$\mc L'$ to $X_{T''}$ are isomorphic \emdash or to put it informally, the
1339	restrictions of $\mc L'$ must agree on a covering of the overlaps.
1340	
1341	Furthermore, a second such sheaf $\mc L_1$ on $X_{T_1}$ represents the
1342	same $T$-point if and only if there is an fppf-covering $T_1'\to T_1\x_T
1343	T'$ such that the pullbacks of $\mc L'$ and $\mc L_1$ to $X_{T_1'}$ are
1344	isomorphic.  Technically, this condition includes the preceding one,
1345	which concerns the case where $T_1=T'$ and $\mc L_1=\mc L'$ since $\mc
1346	L'$ must represent the same $T$-point as itself.  Of course, similar
1347	considerations apply to the Picard functors for the Zariski and \'etale
1348	topologies as well.
1349	
1350	\begin{ex}\label{ex:Alr}
1351	 Given an $S$-scheme $T$ of the form $T=\Spec(A)$ where $A$ is a local
1352	ring, show that the natural maps are isomorphisms
1353		$$\Pic_X(A)\risom\Pic_{X/S}(A)\risom\Pic_{(X/S)\zar}(A)$$
1354	 where $\Pic_X(A):=\Pic_X(T)$, where $\Pic_{X/S}(A):=\Pic_{X/S}(T)$,
1355	and so forth.
1356	
1357	Assume $A$ is Artin local with algebraically closed residue field.  Show
1358		$$\Pic_X(A)\risom\Pic_{(X/S)\et}(A).$$
1359	
1360	Assume $A$ is an algebraically closed  field $k$.  Show
1361		$$\Pic_X(k)\risom\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}(k).$$
1362	  \end{ex}
1363	
1364	\begin{ex}\label{ex:Pfs}
1365	 Show that the natural map
1366		$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar}\to \Pic_{(X/S)\et}$$
1367	 need not be an isomorphism.  Specifically, take $X$ to be the following
1368	 curve  in the real projective plane:
1369		$$X:u^2+v^2+w^2=0 \text{ in }\I P^2_\bb R.$$
1370	 Then $X$ has no $\bb R$-points, but  over the complex numbers $\bb C$,
1371	 there is an isomorphism
1372		$$\vf\:X_{\bb C}\risom\I P^1_{\bb C}.$$
1373	 Show that $\vf^*\mc O(1)$ defines an element of $\Pic_{(X/\bb R)\et}(\bb
1374	R)$, which is not in the image of $\Pic_{(X/\bb R)\zar}(\bb R)$.
1375	 \end{ex}

1377	The main result of this section is the following comparison theorem.
1378	It identifies two useful conditions: the first guarantees that the first
1379	three relative functors can be viewed as subfunctors of the fourth;
1380	together, the two conditions guarantee that all four functors coincide.
1381	The second condition has three successively weaker forms.  Before we can
1382	prove the theorem, we must develop some theory.
1383	
1384	\begin{thm}[Comparison]\label{th:cmp}
1385	 Assume $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds universally; that is, for any
1386	$S$-scheme $T$, the comorphism of $f_T$ is an isomorphism, $\mc O_{\!T}\risom
1387	f_{\smash{T}*}\mc O_{\!X_T}$.
1388	 \smallskip
1389	
1390	\tu{1.}\enspace
1391	 Then the natural maps are injections:
1392		$$\Pic_{X/S}\into \Pic_{(X/S)\zar}\into \Pic_{(X/S)\et}\into
1393		\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1394	
1395	\tu{2.}\enspace All three maps are isomorphisms if also $f$ has a
1396	section; the latter two maps are isomorphisms if also $f$ has a section
1397	locally in the Zariski topology; and the last map is isomorphism if also
1398	$f$ has a section locally in the \'etale topology.
1399	 \end{thm}
1400	
1401	\begin{ex}\label{ex:gpts}
1402	 Assume $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds universally.  Using
1403	Theorem~\ref{th:cmp}, show that its four functors  have the same
1404	geometric points; in other words, for every algebraically closed field
1405	$k$ containing the residue class field of a point of $S$, the $k$-points
1406	of all four functors are, in a natural way, the same.  Show, in fact,
1407	that these $k$-points are just the elements of $\Pic(X_k)$.
1408	
1409	What if $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ does not necessarily hold
1410	universally?
1411	 \end{ex}

1413	\begin{lem}\label{lm:fff}
1414	 Assume $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$.  Then the functor $\mc N\mapsto
1415	f^*\mc N$ is fully-faithful from the category $\mc C$ of locally free
1416	sheaves of finite rank on $S$ to that on $X$.  The essential image is
1417	formed by the sheaves $\mc M$ on $X$ such that \tu{(i)} the image
1418	$f_*\mc M$ is in $\mc C$ and \tu{(ii)} the natural map $f^*f_*\mc
1419	M\to\mc M$ is an isomorphism.
1420	 \end{lem}
1421	
1422	\begin{proof} (Compare with \cite[21.13.2]{EGAIV4}.) For any $\mc
1423	N$ in $\mc C$, there is a string of three natural isomorphisms
1424	  \begin{equation}\label{eq:2a}
1425		\mc N\risom \mc N\ox f_*\mc O_{\!X}\risom
1426		  \mc N\ox f_*f^*\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*f^*\mc N.
1427	  \end{equation}
1428	 The first isomorphism arises by tensor product with the comorphism of
1429	$f$; this comorphism is an isomorphism by hypothesis.  The second
1430	isomorphism arises from the identification $\mc O_{\!X}=f^*\mc O_{\!S}$.  The
1431	third arises from the projection formula.
1432	
1433	For any $\mc N'$ in $\mc C$, also $\SHom(\mc N,\,\mc N')$ is in $\mc C$.
1434	Hence,
1435	 (\ref{eq:2a}) yields an isomorphism
1436		$$\SHom(\mc N,\,\mc N')\risom f_*f^*\SHom(\mc N,\,\mc N').$$
1437	 Now, since $\mc N$ and $\mc N'$ are locally free of finite rank, the
1438	natural map
1439		$$f^*\SHom(\mc N,\,\mc N')\to \SHom(f^*\mc N,\,f^*\mc N')$$
1440	 is an isomorphism locally, so globally.  Hence there is an isomorphism
1441	of groups
1442		$$\Hom(\mc N,\,\mc N')\risom \Hom(f^*\mc N,\,f^*\mc N').$$
1443	 In other words, $\mc N\mapsto f^*\mc N$ is fully-faithful.
1444	
1445	Finally, the essential image consists of the sheaves $\mc M$ that are
1446	isomorphic to those of the form $f^*\mc N$ for some $\mc N$ in $\mc C$.
1447	Given such an $\mc M$ and $\mc N$, there is an isomorphism $f_*\mc
1448	M\simeq \mc N$ owing to (\ref{eq:2a}).  Hence $f_*\mc M$ is in $\mc C$,
1449	and $f^*f_*\mc M\to\mc M$ is an isomorphism locally, so globally; thus
1450	(i) and (ii) hold.  Conversely, if (i) and (ii) hold, then $\mc M$ is,
1451	by definition, in the essential image.
1452	 \end{proof}

1454	\begin{proof}[Proof of Part 1 of Theorem \ref{th:cmp}]
1455	 Given $\lambda\in\Pic_{X/S}(T)$, represent $\lambda$ by an invertible
1456	sheaf $\mc L$ on $X_T$.  Suppose $\lambda$ maps to $0$ in
1457	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}(T)$.  Then there exist an fppf covering $p\:T'\to T$
1458	and an isomorphism $p_X^*\mc L\simeq f_{T'}^*\mc N'$ for some invertible
1459	sheaf $\mc N'$ on $T'$. Hence Lemma~\ref{lm:fff} implies that
1460	$f_{\smash{T'}*}p_X^*\mc L\simeq\mc N'$.  Now,  $p$ is flat, so
1461	$p^*f_{\smash{T}*}\mc L\risom f_{\smash{T'}*}p_X^*\mc L$. So
1462	$p^*f_{\smash{T}*}\mc L\simeq\mc N'$.  Hence $f_{\smash{T}*}\mc L$
1463	is invertible and the natural map $f^*_Tf_{\smash{T}*}\mc L\to\mc L$ is
1464	an isomorphism, as both statements hold after pullback via $p$, which
1465	is faithfully flat.  Therefore, $\lambda=0$.  Thus $\Pic_{X/S}\into
1466	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.
1467	
1468	The rest is formal.  Indeed, take the last injection, and form the
1469	associated sheaves in the Zariski topology. This operation is exact by
1470	general (Grothendieck) topology, and $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$ remains the
1471	same, as it is already a Zariski sheaf.  Thus $\Pic_{(X/S)\zar}\into
1472	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  Similarly, $\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\into
1473	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.
1474	
1475	Alternatively, we can avoid the use of Lemma~\ref{lm:fff} by starting
1476	from the fact that $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$ is the sheaf associated to
1477	$\Pic_X$, rather than to $\Pic_{X/S}$.  This way, we may assume $\mc
1478	N'=\mc O_{T'}$.  Then $f_{\smash{T'}*}p_X^*\mc L\simeq\mc N'$ because
1479	$\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$ holds universally. We now proceed
1480	just as before.
1481	 \end{proof}

1483	\begin{dfn}\label{df:rgd}
1484	 Assume $f$ has a section $g$, so $fg=1$.  Let $T$ be an $S$-scheme,
1485	and $\mc L$ a sheaf on $X_T$.  Then a $g$-{\it rigidification} of $\mc
1486	L$ is the choice of an isomorphism $u\:\mc O_{\!T}\risom g^*_T\mc L$,
1487	assuming one exists.
1488	 \end{dfn}
1489	
1490	\begin{lem}\label{lm:idn}
1491	 Assume $f$ has a section $g$, and let $T$ be an $S$-scheme.  Form the
1492	group of isomorphism classes of pairs  $(\mc L,u)$ where $\mc L$ is an
1493	invertible sheaf on $X_T$ and $u$ is a $g$-rigidification of $\mc
1494	L$.  Then this group is carried isomorphically onto $\Pic_{X/S}(T)$ by
1495	the homomorphism $\rho$ defined by $\rho(\mc L,u):=\mc L$. 
1496	 \end{lem}
1497	 \begin{proof}
1498	 Given  $\lambda$ in $\Pic_{X/S}(T)$, represent  $\lambda$ by an
1499	invertible sheaf $\mc M$ on $X_T$.  Set $\mc L:=\mc M\ox(f_T^*g^*_T\mc
1500	M)^{-1}$. Then $\mc L$ too represents $\lambda$.  Also
1501	$g^*_T\mc L =g_T^*\mc M\ox g_T^*f_T^*g_T^*\mc M^{-1}$.  Now,
1502	$g_T^*f_T^*=1$ as $fg=1$.  Hence the natural isomorphism $g_T^*\mc
1503	M\ox(g_T^*\mc M)^{-1}\risom \mc \mc O_{\!T}$ induces a $g$-rigidification of
1504	$\mc L$.  Thus $\rho$ is surjective.
1505	
1506	To prove $\rho$ is injective, let $(\mc L,u)$ represent an element of
1507	its kernel. Then there exist an invertible sheaf $\mc N$ on $T$ and an
1508	isomorphism $v\:\mc L\risom f^*_T\mc N$.  Set $w:=g_T^*v\circ u$, so
1509	$w\:\mc O_{\!T}\risom g^*_T\mc L\risom\mc N$.  Now, a map of pairs is just a
1510	map $w'$ of the first components such that $g^*_Tw'$ is compatible with
1511	the two $g$-rigidifications.  So $v\:(\mc L,u)\risom(f^*_T\mc N,w)$ and
1512	$f^*_Tw\:(\mc O_{\!X_T},1)\risom(f^*_T\mc N,w)$. Thus $\rho$ is injective.
1513	\end{proof}

1515	\begin{lem}\label{lm:aut}
1516	 Assume $f$ has a section $g$, and assume $\mc O_{\!S}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X}$
1517	holds universally.  Let $T$ be an $S$-scheme, $\mc L$ an invertible
1518	sheaf on $X_T$, and $u$ a $g$-rigidification of $\mc L$.  Then every
1519	automorphism of the pair $(\mc L,u)$ is trivial.
1520	 \end{lem}
1521	 \begin{proof}
1522	 An automorphism of $(\mc L,u)$ is just an automorphism $v\:\mc L\risom
1523	\mc L$ such that $g_T^*v\circ u\:\mc O_{\!T}\risom g^*_T\mc L\risom g^*_T\mc
1524	L$ is equal to $u$.  But then $g_T^*v=1$.  Now,
1525		$$v\in\Hom(\mc L,\mc L)=\uH^0(\SHom(\mc L,\mc L))
1526		=\uH^0(\mc O_{\!X_T})=\uH^0(\mc O_{T});$$
1527	 the middle equation holds since the natural map $O_{\!X_T}\to\SHom(\mc
1528	L,\mc L)$  is locally an isomorphism, so globally one, and the
1529	last equation holds since $\mc O_{\!T}\risom f_*\mc O_{\!X_T}$.  But
1530	$g_T^*v=1$.  Therefore,  $v=1$.
1531	 \end{proof}

1533	\begin{proof}[Proof of Part 2 of Theorem \ref{th:cmp}]
1534	 Suppose $f$ has a section $g$.  Owing to Part 1, it suffices to prove
1535	that every $\lambda\in \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}(T)$ lies in $\Pic_{X/S}(T)$.

1537	T$ is an fppf covering.  Then there is an fppf covering $T''\to
1538	T'\x_TT'$ such that the two pullbacks of $\lambda'$ to $X_{T''}$ are
1539	equal.  We may assume $T''\risom T'\x_T T'$ because $\Pic_{X/S}$ is separated
1540	for the fppf topology, again owing to Part 1.

1544	is a $g$-rigidification of $\mc L'$.  Furthermore, on $X_{T'\x T'}$, there
1545	is an isomorphism $v'$ from the pullback of $(\mc L',u')$ via the first
1546	projection onto the pullback via the second.

1549	$v'_{ij}$ denote the pullback of $v'$ via the projection to the $i$th

1560	since both source and target are sheaves in the Zariski topology and a map

1562	$\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\risom \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$ if $f$ has a section locally

1566	\begin{rmk}\label{rk:coh}
1567	 There is another way to prove Theorem~\ref{th:cmp}.  This way is more
1568	sophisticated, and  yields more information, which we won't need.
1569	Here is the idea.
1570	
1571	Recall \cite[Ex.~III, 4.5, p.~224]{Ha83} that, for any ringed space $R$,
1572	there is a natural isomorphism
1573	 \begin{equation}\label{eq:2b}
1574		\Pic(R) = \uH^1(R,\mc O_{\!R}^*).
1575	  \end{equation}
1576	 Now, given any $S$-scheme  $T$, form the presheaf $T'\mapsto
1577	\uH^1(X_{T'},\mc O_{\!X_{T'}}^*)$ on $T$.  Its associated sheaf is
1578	\cite[Prp.~III, 8.1, p.~250]{Ha83} simply $\tu R^1f_{\smash{T}*}\mc
1579	O_{\!X_T}^*$.  Therefore,
1580	  \begin{equation}\label{eq:2e}

1596	O_{\!T}^*)\risom\uH^1(T,f_{\smash{T}*}\mc O_{\!X_T}^*)$.  Hence the beginning

1602	inverse of the map $\uH^p(T,f_{\smash{T}*}\mc O_{\!X_T}^*)\to\uH^p(X_T,\mc
1603	O_{\!X_T}^*)$ induced by $f$.  So the latter is injective.  Hence,

1606	
1607	The preceding argument works for the \'etale and fppf topologies
1608	too with little change.  First of all, consider the functor
1609		$$\Gm(T):=\uH^0(T,\mc O_{\!T}^*).$$
1610	 Let $u$ be an indeterminate.  Then $\Gm(T)$ is representable by the
1611	$S$-scheme
1612		$$\Gm:=\Spec(\mc O_{\!S}[u,u^{-1}]).$$
1613	 Indeed, giving an $S$-map $T\to\Gm$ is the same as giving an

1615	O_{\!S})[u,u^{-1}]$ to $\uH^0(T,\mc O_{\!T})$, and giving such a

48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78	
79	## What the dispatcher will use this for
80	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>
81	
82	## Seeds (optional)
83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.
144	
145	5. **Write the pointer file** `references/<slug>.md` (format below) in BOTH the success and failure cases:
146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."
148	
149	6. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing the slug.
150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.
152	
153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.
164	
165	## Pointer file format
166	
167	`references/<slug>.md` is an index card, not a summary. Keep it short.
168	
169	### Success — file(s) downloaded and verified
170	
171	```markdown
172	# <Title>
173	
174	## Citation
175	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
176	
177	## Slug
178	<slug>
179	
180	## Retrieval status
181	RETRIEVED — <date>
182	
183	## Local source files
184	- references/<slug>.pdf — PDF, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>
185	- references/<slug>.tex — LaTeX source, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>   ← if obtained
186	(List every file you saved and the EXACT URL it came from. These successful links are
187	the most important field — a later planner uses them to re-fetch or double-check.)
188	
189	## Why this source
190	<2-3 sentences: which Archon chapter / strategy decision will use this. Copy from the directive's "What the dispatcher will use this for".>
191	
192	## Contents map
193	<A table of contents / section map pointing INTO the downloaded file, so a planner knows where to look. Use the source's own numbering. NO paraphrase of the mathematics — just locations.>
194	
195	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>
196	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>   ← relevant to this directive
197	- (deep depth only) Theorem/Lemma <number> — §<n>, p.<page>
198	
199	## Caveats
200	<Things the dispatcher should know: paywalled-but-open-copy-found, unusual conventions, known errata, contested results, edition differences. If none, write "None".>
201	
202	## Quality / provenance
203	<one or two sentences: is this the definitive reference or one of several? Where exactly did the file come from, and how confident are you it is the authoritative version?>
204	```
205	
206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).
209	
210	```markdown
211	# <Title>
212	
213	## Citation
214	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
215	
216	## Slug
217	<slug>
218	
219	## Retrieval status
220	NOT_RETRIEVED — <date>. No local source file exists. DO NOT cite this as `(read from …)`.
221	
222	## Sources tried
223	- <url 1> — <what happened: 404 / paywall / HTML-only login page / corrupt file>
224	- <url 2> — <…>
225	- <search query used> — <no open copy among results>
226	
227	## Why it matters / what to do next
228	<which chapter/decision needed this, and the dispatcher's options: find another route, pick a different source, or treat the dependent block as gated on retrieval>
229	
230	<!-- NO contents map, NO abstract, NO recalled statements. There is nothing verified to record. -->
231	```
232	
233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
270	
271	```markdown
272	# Reference Retriever Report
273	
274	## Slug
275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```
299	
300	## Return value
301	
302	Your final assistant message:
303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.

\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean

% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
% pullback functor $\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)$ along the
% projection $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ of a relative curve, and the
% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
% $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$
% as a functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$. This chapter is gated on
% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ as a sheaf), A.2.*
% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).

% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$ as a
% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$. The abelian-group refinement
% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of $g^*$ as soon as a
% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
% does not yet exist.

\section{Setup and motivation}
\label{sec:lbp_setup}

Throughout this chapter, fix a base field $k$ and a smooth proper geometrically
integral curve $C$ over $k$ (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
constructions are functorial in $C$ and do not formally use these hypotheses
until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
chapters). A test object is a $k$-scheme $T$; the relative curve over $T$ is
$C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T$, with $\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C$ the
companion projection. Both $\pi_T$ and $\pi_C$ are flat (because base change of
flat morphisms is flat and $C/k$ is flat since $k$ is a field).

The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
product: the Picard group $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the total space, and the
Picard group $\Pic(T)$ of the base. The pullback functor $\pi_T^*$ sends a line
bundle on $T$ to its base-change line bundle on $C \times_k T$; the
\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
\[
  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
\]
is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
is the universal Set-valued functor on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ that ``forgets line
bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).

\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}

\begin{definition}[Line bundle on a product]
  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
  Let $k$ be a base field, $C$ a $k$-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
  geometrically integral curve), and $T$ a $k$-scheme. A
  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve $C \times_k T$} is an invertible
  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module, that is, an
  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module $\mathcal{L}$ such that there exists an
  open cover $\{U_i\}$ of $C \times_k T$ on which each restriction
  $\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}$ is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
  they are isomorphic as $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-modules. The set of
  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
  $\Pic(C \times_k T)$. In Kleiman's notation with $X = C$ and $S = \Spec k$,
  this is exactly the value
  $\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)$ of the absolute Picard functor.
\end{definition}

\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}

If $f : Y \to Z$ is a morphism of schemes and $\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
$\mathcal{O}_Z$-module, the pullback $f^*\mathcal{M}$ is an invertible
$\mathcal{O}_Y$-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising $\mathcal{M}$
trivialises $f^*\mathcal{M}$, and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
specialise it to the projection $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and the
$\mathcal{O}_T$-Picard group.

\begin{definition}[Pullback along the projection]
  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
  %  [\dots]
  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
  % sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
  %     $$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
  Let $C/k$ and $T/k$ be as in
  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$
  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along $\pi_T$} is the
  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
  in this chapter)
  \[
    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
  \]
  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible $\mathcal{O}_T$-module
  $\mathcal{N}$ to the isomorphism class of $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}$, an invertible
  $\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}$-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
  defined because pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules respects isomorphism, and is
  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
  $\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C$ that we quotient by in the definition of
  the relative Picard presheaf.
\end{definition}

\section{Composition of pullbacks}
\label{sec:lbp_compose}

\begin{lemma}[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
  % includes composition)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. Write
  $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$ and $\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'$ for the
  canonical projections, and write
  $g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T$ for the
  base change of $g$ along $C/k$. Then on line bundles we have the equality of
  pullback maps
  \[
    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
  \]
  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
  \[
    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
  \]
  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
  $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T$.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
  The square
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
    \end{array}
  \]
  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
  composites $\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}$ holds on the nose. Pullback of
  $\mathcal{O}$-modules is functorial, so for any $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$
  there is a canonical isomorphism
  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}$
  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
  $(\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}$
  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying $\mathcal{O}$-module
  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
  set-valued maps stated above.
\end{proof}

\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}

We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.

\begin{theorem}[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
  % topologies  by
  %     $$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
  %  \end{dfn}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let $T$ be a
  $k$-scheme. The subset
  \[
    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
  \]
  is the image of the pullback map $\pi_T^*$
  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
  class and is closed under the multiplication of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$ (so it is
  a subgroup). Define
  \[
    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
  \]
  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes $[\mathcal{L}]$ of
  line bundles $\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)$ under the relation
  $\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'$ iff
  $\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T$.
  Then $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a well-defined assignment from
  $k$-schemes to sets, and the natural map
  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is a surjection of sets.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
% not a translation of an external argument.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:pullback_along_projection}
  The subset $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ is a subgroup of $\Pic(C \times_k T)$
  because the pullback map $\pi_T^*$ is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
  $\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T$ is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
  $\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ is the canonical quotient map
  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
\end{proof}

\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}

\begin{theorem}[Pullback descends through the quotient]
  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
  \uses{def:line_bundle_on_product, def:pullback_along_projection, lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  %     $$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
  %  [\dots]
  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
  %     $$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in $T$;
  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
  Let $C/k$ be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
  $g : T' \to T$ be a morphism of $k$-schemes. The pullback maps
  $g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')$ and
  $g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')$ make the diagram
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
    \end{array}
  \]
  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore $g_C^*$ sends
  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$ and hence induces a
  unique set map
  \[
    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
  \]
  through the quotients of
  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
  $g \mapsto g^\sharp$ satisfies $\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ and
  $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ for any composable
  $h : T'' \to T'$ and $g : T' \to T$; thus
  $T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)$ extends to a functor
  $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$.
\end{theorem}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_compose, thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
  $g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*$ of
  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore $g_C^*$ maps the image
  $H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)$ into the image
  $H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')$: an element
  $\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T$ with $\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)$ has
  $g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}$. Hence
  $g_C^*$ induces a unique map of quotient sets
  $g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}$ by the
  universal property of the quotient set
  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
  $\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}$ is immediate from
  $\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}$ for pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules. The
  composition law $(g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp$ follows from
  the corresponding statement for $\mathcal{O}$-module pullback
  $(g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*$ (passing through the quotient is functorial).
  Hence $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$ is a presheaf.
\end{proof}

\section{Lean encoding}
\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}

The Lean target file is the new
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
Lean targets as follows.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on $C \times_k T$. Mathlib provides
    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
    the product scheme.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
    groups induced by $\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T$. The Mathlib backbone is
    the pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules
    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
    structural existence of the quotient set
    $\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T$. Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
    $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}$. The naturality square
    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
    quotient-induced map.
\end{itemize}

No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
prover wishes to perform on an affine open of $C \times_k T$.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:lbp_out_of_scope}

This chapter packages \emph{only} the line-bundle pullback functor on a
relative curve and the well-definedness + functoriality of the set-valued
quotient $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$. The following downstream constructions live in
separate sibling chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Group structure on $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}$} (A.1.c) --- the
    quotient is here treated as set-valued; the abelian-group enhancement
    (and the \'etale-sheafification step that turns the presheaf into a
    representable functor) belongs to the planned chapter
